options.explanation: When the pattern has groups, findall returns a list of tuples — one tuple per match, with one element per capture group.
That's what findall returns when the pattern has *no* groups. As soon as you add parenthesized groups, the return value switches to a list of tuples.
findall always returns a list, not a single tuple. It collects every match in the string.
findall returns a list of all matches, not just the first one. Use re.search if you only want the first match.

questions.question: Which of these patterns correctly validates that a string is *exactly* five digits and nothing else?

options.explanation: The ^ anchor requires the match to start at the beginning of the string; the $ anchor requires it to end there. Together they ensure nothing else can appear.
Without anchors, re.search will find a five-digit sequence anywhere in the string. '78664 Main St' would pass even though it's not a zip code.
^ anchors the start, but without $ the pattern matches any string that *begins* with five digits. '786641234' would pass.
$ anchors the end, but without ^ the pattern matches any string that *ends* with five digits. 'abc78664' would pass.

questions.question: What is the difference between `re.match` and `re.search`?

options.text: re.match only looks at the start of the string; re.search looks anywhere.
options.explanation: re.match anchors at the beginning — it returns None if the pattern doesn't start at position 0. re.search scans the whole string and returns the first match wherever it appears.

options.text: re.match returns all matches; re.search returns only the first.
options.explanation: Both return a single match object (or None). To get all matches, use re.findall.

options.text: re.search is case-insensitive by default; re.match is not.
options.explanation: Both are case-sensitive by default. Pass re.IGNORECASE (or re.I) as a flag to either function to make them case-insensitive.

options.text: re.match requires a compiled pattern; re.search accepts a raw string.
options.explanation: Both functions accept either a raw string pattern or a compiled re.Pattern object.
